topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The box arrived on my doorstep just as the rain started hammering against the windowpane. It was a stark arrangement of deep purple irises and pale, almost translucent lilies, all perfectly placed in stark white wrapping paper. There was no note. No signature. Just the silent, heavy weight of the flowers resting on the welcome mat. I stared at them, my breath catching in my throat. My hands, usually steady, felt clammy as I tentatively reached out, my fingertips brushing the cool, velvety petals. A knot tightened in my chest, a sudden, sharp awareness that something was profoundly out of place in the quiet monotony of my evening. I backed away slowly, my eyes darting toward the darkened hallway, wondering who, or what, had orchestrated this silent offering.

[story 2]

I found the bouquet tucked behind the heavy curtain in the living room. They were vibrant—a riot of orange and shocking pink roses—and utterly devoid of context. I picked them up, the stems cool against my palm. My jaw clenched, and I could feel a strange pressure building behind my eyes. Every instinct screamed at me to throw them away, to discard the intrusion immediately. Yet, I couldn't bring myself to move. I turned the arrangement over and over, searching for a hidden message, a clue to the sender’s intent. The silence in the apartment seemed amplified, pressing in on me, making the simple act of breathing feel labored. I paced three steps to the kitchen and back, my movements jerky and uncoordinated.

[story 3]

When I opened the door, the scent of cut grass and heavy perfume hit me first. Inside, sitting on the antique side table, were a dozen perfect, cream-colored peonies. There was no accompanying paper, no whisper of a name. My gaze swept over the arrangement, lingering on the way the light caught the delicate edges of the petals. A cold trickle of sweat traced a path down my spine. I stood frozen just inside the threshold, my heart beginning a frantic, uneven rhythm against my ribs. I wanted to demand an explanation, to demand *why* this unsolicited gesture had been made, but my voice caught in my throat, leaving only a shallow, ragged sound. I just stood there, rooted to the spot, waiting for the air to move again.
